mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW X3 30d engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned X3 30d engine typically costs £1,800–£8,500 supply only depending on generation. M57-era engines sit at the lower end (£1,800–£4,500), while B57 units run £3,500–£8,500. Fitting adds £650–£1,500. Used N57 engines have been listed from £1,295 upwards, with some suppliers offering engines at £2,300–£4,000. A complete reconditioned engine for the X3 starts from around €3,990. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW X3 30d engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on which of the three engine generations your X3 30d has. The M57 (E83, 2003–2010) is widely regarded as one of BMW's most durable diesel engines — it won International Engine of the Year multiple times. The N57 (F25, 2010–2017) is more refined but less reliable — timing chain and bearing failures have been reported. The B57 (G01, 2017+) is a significant improvement with robust chain design, but EGR cooler and turbo issues remain common. Check the Era Map above for your specific car.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW X3 30d?

acceptedAnswer.text: Swirl flap failure on the M57 (E83), timing chain and bearing failure on the N57 (F25), and EGR cooler leaks, DPF blockages, and turbocharger failures on the B57 (G01). Oil leaks are common across all generations. The M57 is the most durable overall.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW X3 30d engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and generation. Against a well-kept £6,500 F25 X3 30d, a £4,500 recon is 69% of the car's value — borderline. Against a tired £4,500 example, the same recon exceeds our 70% BMW threshold — get a scrap valuation first. For G01 B57-engined cars (2017+), higher vehicle values (£15,000+) make replacement more viable.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW X3 30d engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. As a rule of thumb: E83 (2003–2010) is M57D30 (204–218 bhp), F25 (2010–2017) is N57D30O1 (258 bhp), G01 (2017–present) is B57D30O0 (261–286 bhp). Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the N57 timing chain, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — if caught early. A preventative chain replacement (£1,200–£3,000) is far cheaper than a catastrophic failure requiring a full engine replacement. Many specialists recommend preventative chain replacement at 100,000 miles regardless of symptoms. At the first sign of rattling or knocking, book an inspection immediately. Regular oil changes every 8,000–10,000 miles are critical for N57 longevity.

rows.bestFor: Insurance jobs, warranty claims (modern cars only)

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the current B57 generation, the most common quote request today. Older generations: see the Era Map above for M57 and N57-specific pricing, which runs lower. Used N57 engines for the F25 X3 have been listed from £1,295 upwards, with some suppliers offering engines at £2,300–£4,000 with fitting at £495–£1,150. A complete reconditioned engine for the X3 starts from around €3,990, with fitting from €5,490.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an X3 30d engine typically takes 12–16 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent). The six-cylinder diesel's longitudinal layout and complex emissions systems add labour time.

cta.label: → Compare X3 30d engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: N57 Timing Chain & Bearing Failure (F25 2010–2017)
problems.symptoms: Rattling or metallic noise on cold start, metallic knocking, oil pressure loss warning, engine warning light. Some owners report a whining noise from the timing chain area.
problems.typicalMileage: 100,000–150,000 miles — chain stretch can occur by 100,000 miles.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only if caught early; catastrophic failure requires full engine replacement.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattle or knocking, book an inspection today.

problems.recommendation: The N57 shares timing chain components with the N47 — chain stretch and guide wear are a concern. By 100,000 miles, many specialists recommend preventative chain replacement. If the chain snaps or tensioner fails, it's an interference engine — piston-valve contact means catastrophic damage. Bearing failures have also been reported in N57 engines, with some owners experiencing bottom-end failure. At the first sign of rattling or knocking, book an inspection immediately. Regular oil changes every 8,000–10,000 miles are critical for longevity.

problems.title: M57 Swirl Flap Failure (E83 2003–2010)
problems.symptoms: Loss of power, rough idle, engine warning light, occasional rattling from the intake manifold, black smoke.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; if a flap detaches, it can enter a cylinder and cause catastrophic engine damage.
problems.recommendation: The M57's swirl flaps can break off and get sucked into the engine, causing severe damage to the cylinders, pistons and valves. Preventive blanking is cheap, effective, and removes the risk entirely with no noticeable performance loss. Many specialists recommend blanking as a matter of course on any high-mileage M57. The flaps can also get clogged with carbon deposits, restricting airflow.

problems.title: EGR Cooler & Turbocharger Issues (B57 — G01 2017–present)
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss with no visible external leak, white exhaust smoke, engine warning light, reduced performance, black smoke, unusual noises.
problems.typicalMileage: Any mileage — EGR issues reported from 40,000–60,000 miles.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; ignoring coolant loss can lead to overheating and engine damage.

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if symptoms appear.

problems.recommendation: The B57 is a significant improvement over the N57, with timing chain issues being "exceedingly rare". However, EGR cooler leaks — where coolant leaks into the intake, causing white smoke or overheating — are a known problem. Turbocharger failures and DPF blockages are also common. Check whether your X3 has had any EGR-related recall work completed. Regular oil changes and quality fuel are essential for B57 longevity.

buyingChecks.title: Buying This X3 30d? Check These First
buyIf: * E83 M57 with swirl flap blanking completed — the most durable X3 diesel
* F25 N57 with documented timing chain replacement (especially over 100,000 miles)
* G01 B57 with EGR recall completed and full service history
* Full service history with oil changes every 8,000–10,000 miles
avoidIf: * N57 with cold-start rattle and no chain history — timing chain or bearing risk
* No proof of swirl flap blanking on an M57 — the risk is real
* B57 with coolant loss or white smoke — EGR cooler failure
* Poor service history on any generation — these engines are unforgiving of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The X3 30d badge spans three generations of BMW six-cylinder diesel — from the bulletproof M57 (E83) to the refined but riskier N57 (F25) and the modern B57 (G01). The M57 is widely considered the most durable. The N57 needs timing chain vigilance. The B57 is a significant improvement but EGR and turbo issues remain. With the 70% BMW scrap threshold, a £4,500 recon on a £6,500 F25 X3 30d is 69% — borderline. On a sub-£5,000 example, think carefully.

cta.label: → Compare X3 30d engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW X3 30d (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling or knocking on startup (N57 timing chain / bearing warning)
* Service History — Including which engine era (M57/N57/B57) and confirmed oil-interval discipline
* Swirl Flap Blanking — Any evidence of blanking on M57? Essential preventative work
* Timing Chain Documentation — Any evidence of chain replacement on N57 (especially over 100,000 miles)
* Coolant Check — Check level and condition — B57 EGR cooler leaks are common
* Smoke Check — White smoke could indicate EGR coolant leak; black smoke could indicate turbo or injector issues
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
